frozen_components: global word embeddings (used as input to projection)
architecture.summary: MeKi augments each Transformer layer with a parallel memory-based expert module that retrieves token-level knowledge vectors from a layer-specific embedding table and injects them into the hidden state via low-rank gated fusion. During training, a non-linear projection (SwiGLU) synthesizes dynamic features from global word embeddings; these projections are re-parameterized post-training into static lookup tables offloaded to ROM, eliminating inference-time FLOPs while preserving parameter count in RAM.

biblio.title: MeKi: Memory-based Expert Knowledge Injection for Efficient LLM Scaling

claims.dataset: 10 benchmarks (ARC-E/C, BoolQ, COPA, HellaSwag, LAMBADA, OBQA, PIQA, SciQ, WinoGrande)
claims.statement: MeKi-1.7B achieves an average zero-shot benchmark score of 59.7, matching the 4B dense model's score of 60.5, while maintaining a 2.26x advantage in decoding speed
On ARC-Challenge, MeKi-1.7B achieves 37.9, surpassing the 1.7B baseline by +3.5 points and matching the 4B baseline (38.0)
On SciQ, MeKi-1.7B reaches 85.4, outperforming the baseline by 4.8 points

claims.dataset: 10 benchmarks (ARC-E/C, BoolQ, COPA, HellaSwag, LAMBADA, OBQA, PIQA, SciQ, WinoGrande)
claims.statement: MeKi-1.7B outperforms PLE by 2.7 points and Engram by 1.8 points on average zero-shot performance
MeKi maintains the same generation speed (token/s) as the dense baseline on Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite with KV cache length 10K
Re-parameterization strategy merges dynamic projections into static lookup tables, achieving zero inference latency overhead by offloading knowledge to ROM
MeKi exhibits systematically lower KL divergence across all layers compared to baseline, indicating accelerated prediction convergence toward final output distribution
Placing MeKi parallel to FFN achieves best performance; positioning after Attention or FFN leads to information bottleneck and performance drop
